one_line_claim: WildClawBench is a 60-task native-runtime benchmark arguing that current CLI agents still struggle on realistic long-horizon multimodal and bilingual work: the best OpenClaw score is 62.2%, and changing only the harness can move a model by up to 18 points.
what_they_tested: They evaluate 19 frontier models on 60 human-authored tasks across productivity, code intelligence, social interaction, search/retrieval, creative synthesis, and safety alignment. OpenClaw is the main harness; four models are also tested across OpenClaw, Claude Code, Codex, and Hermes Agent. They add analyses for multimodal vs pure-text tasks, English vs Chinese prompts, time budgets, GPT 5.4 thinking modes, category-level skill augmentation, failure modes, repeated-run variance for four models, and a small human-vs-GPT judge agreement study.
strongest_point: The paper attacks the right evaluation gap: not just final answers, but real tool use, generated artifacts, side effects, runtime logs, and harness behavior. The Dockerized setup, post-run mounting of grading assets, hybrid deterministic/state/LLM judging, and multiple-harness comparison make this substantially more useful than another short synthetic agent benchmark.
weakest_point: Benchmark validity is asserted more strongly than it is proven. The suite has only 60 curated tasks, was filtered for inter-model score separation, uses GPT 5.4 as semantic judge while GPT 5.4 is also a tested model, validates judging on only five sampled tasks, and reports repeated-run uncertainty for only four models. The headline results are interesting, but should be read as properties of this curated suite and harness stack, not as a general measurement of real-world agent capability.
missing_baselines: Time-limited human expert performance on the same tasks, to expose task ambiguity, realistic completion time, and a ceiling score.
A no-tool or final-answer-only LLM baseline, to quantify how much the native runtime and tools actually add.
Simple scripted or heuristic baselines for deterministic extraction, search, scheduling, and file-generation tasks.
More established open agent scaffolds under matched tools, such as OpenHands, SWE-agent, aider, or comparable browser/OS agents where compatible.
A judge-free deterministic/state-only subset reported separately, so readers can see how much of the leaderboard depends on semantic LLM judging.
missing_ablations: Swap GPT 5.4 for other LLM/VLM judges and larger human panels; report correlations, confidence intervals, and disagreement examples.
Show how the model-filtering threshold max pairwise score gap >= 0.2 changes task composition and leaderboard conclusions.
Run more than three trials for all major models, especially the top models, and report uncertainty on Table 2.
Ablate harness components separately: system prompt, tool schema, context-management policy, timeout policy, and output-recovery logic.
Separate frozen local resources from live web/API dependencies to measure reproducibility under external-service drift.
Add skills one at a time and by task relevance rather than as top-download category bundles; the current bundle results mix helpful scaffolding with large regressions.
Test budget and thinking-mode sensitivity beyond GPT 5.4, since the paper's own results show timeouts can dominate agent performance.
red_flags: The task filtering rule keeps tasks with at least a 0.2 pilot-model score gap, which can bias the benchmark toward discriminating among the chosen pilot models rather than representing a natural deployment distribution.
The comparison table against prior benchmarks uses coarse full/partial/no labels; those judgments are partly subjective and not supported by empirical porting of tasks across benchmarks.
The phrase 'real tools rather than mock services' is too broad: social-interaction tasks still use mocked email/chat/calendar participants and APIs, even if the agent runs in a native CLI container.

GPT 5.4 is both the semantic judge and one of the evaluated systems, creating a possible judge-style bias; the human agreement check is small and reports deviations but not robust statistical agreement.
Main leaderboard numbers for many of the 19 models appear to rely on limited runs, while the three-run variance table covers only four representative models.
OpenRouter routing plus rapidly changing proprietary model names make exact replication fragile unless the released code pins exact model IDs, provider routes, temperatures, and retry behavior.

The skill ablation shows severe category regressions, such as GPT 5.4 dropping 48.5 points on Social Interaction with skills, but the paper gives little diagnosis of whether skills changed behavior, tool safety, or grading compatibility.
positive_signals: The evaluation looks at artifacts, environment state, side effects, transcripts, and logs, not just final text answers.
Ground-truth and grading-only assets are mounted only after the agent exits, which directly addresses leakage risk.
The paper reports a release of task specifications, code, containerized workspaces, grading code, and harness configurations.
The harness comparison is valuable because it shows the scaffold is part of the evaluated system, not a neutral wrapper.
The benchmark includes safety tasks embedded in normal workflows, including prompt injection, leaked credentials, malicious skill injection, and destructive-command lures.
Appendices list all task modalities and include representative full prompts, expected behavior, and grading criteria.
The paper includes useful secondary analyses: time budgets, thinking modes, skills, bilingual gaps, failure modes, tool-use profiles, and repeated-run variance for a subset.
verdict: A strong Paper Radio candidate because it makes a concrete, timely claim about agent evaluation: harnesses, time budgets, tools, and side effects are part of the benchmarked system. The contribution is credible and useful, especially if the released artifacts work. The skeptical angle is that the paper's headline 'real-world' and leaderboard claims rest on a small, curated, partly LLM-judged suite with limited uncertainty reporting. Discuss it as a serious benchmark paper, not as definitive evidence of general agent competence.
citations: Abstract and Sec. 3.2: WildClawBench has 60 human-authored tasks, 36 English and 24 Chinese, 26 multimodal and 34 pure text; average budget is 881 seconds, and Claude Opus 4.6 averages 8.5 minutes and 26 tool calls.
Sec. 3.3: task curation uses a pilot-model discriminability filter retaining tasks with max pairwise score gap >= 0.2, followed by expert human filtering and refinement.

Sec. 3.4 and Sec. 4.1: each run executes in a Docker container under OpenClaw, Claude Code, Codex, or Hermes Agent; grading combines deterministic checks, state audits, and GPT 5.4 LLM/VLM judging, with grading-only assets mounted after the agent exits.
Table 2: under OpenClaw, Claude Opus 4.7 scores 62.2 overall, GPT 5.5 scores 58.2, and the full leaderboard ranges from 19.3 to 62.2.
Table 3: harness choice materially changes scores; MiMo V2 Pro moves from 29.9 with Claude Code to 48.1 with Hermes Agent.
Table 4: skill augmentation is mixed; GPT 5.4 improves 5.2 points overall but drops 48.5 points on Social Interaction.
Table 5: GPT 5.4 high-thinking mode scores 45.0 with 15 timeout tasks, worse than low-thinking mode at 50.4 with 4 timeout tasks.
Appendix B: the authors acknowledge single-turn-only tasks, limited 60-task coverage, and light representation of GUI-heavy desktop control and specialist domains.
Tables 10 and 11: repeated-run variance is reported for four models across three runs; GPT-based judge validation samples five LLM-judged tasks with two human evaluators.
